quote: No significant learning occurs without a significant relationship.
through_line: The midweek brief is about proximity — how close AI is getting to the spaces we thought were distinctly human: companionship, education, emotional attunement. The Ada Lovelace Institute puts concrete shape to what AI companions are doing to people who are lonely or vulnerable. The Issues piece asks whether K–12 education has the conceptual vocabulary to respond. Noema’s companion piece argues there is a path to AI that embeds care rather than just compliance. Search and discovery don’t disappear in this picture, but they look different from the inside.

articles.title: AI companions: what are the risks and opportunities?
articles.pitch: The Ada Lovelace Institute surveys the emerging landscape of AI companion apps — the tools people use to manage loneliness, mental health, and emotional connection — and asks what we actually know about their effects. The risks are real and under-researched; the opportunities are also real and often dismissed. A balanced treatment of one of the quieter but more significant social experiments currently underway.

articles.title: LLM Visibility: What It Is and How to Build It
articles.pitch: Backlinko’s guide to LLM visibility makes the case that being cited by AI language models is a distinct challenge from traditional SEO — one that requires understanding how models are trained and updated, not just how search algorithms rank pages. Practically useful for anyone managing web presence in an environment where ChatGPT and Perplexity are becoming entry points.

articles.title: AI Search and Destination Discovery: What’s Changing
articles.pitch: Think Digital Travel and Tourism examines specifically how AI search is affecting the way travellers discover destinations — which intermediaries are gaining, which are losing, and what it means for a DMO’s content strategy when the old model of search ranking no longer applies. Timely, sector-specific, and grounded in current evidence.

articles.title: K–12 Education in the Age of AI
articles.pitch: Shuchi Grover writes in Issues in Science and Technology that the conversation about AI in K–12 education has been happening mostly without a clear picture of what children actually need to learn about AI — not just to use it, but to understand it. Sharp on the gap between policy aspiration and curriculum reality, with concrete directions for closing it.

articles.title: Human-Compatible AI
articles.pitch: A philosophical piece arguing that the goal of AI alignment should be expanded beyond safety and accuracy to include something like care — that human-compatible AI is not just AI that doesn’t harm us, but AI oriented toward human flourishing in a richer sense. Less utopian than it sounds; the argument is grounded in the limits of current alignment frameworks.

articles.title: The scientific discoveries that brought us joy in 2025
articles.pitch: Science News’s end-of-year collection of discoveries that brought genuine delight — not the biggest breakthroughs or most consequential findings, but the ones that made researchers stop and feel wonder. A midweek antidote to the density of the other pieces, and a reminder that science is also a practice of astonishment.
